# The Mold Report — Full Content Archive

This file contains the full editorial text of every published article on https://themoldreport.org. Use this for one-shot ingestion. Each article is canonical at https://themoldreport.org/a/{id}.html with a Markdown companion at https://themoldreport.org/a/{id}.md. Original reporting belongs to the linked source.

========================================================================
## Military Families Sound Alarm Over Toxic Mold in Base Housing

Published: 2026-04-30
Category: news
Source: pbs.org
Original: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/show/families-sound-alarm-on-toxic-mold-in-military-housing
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/03a74f2154ce.html
Tags: mold, housing, military, indoor air quality, policy

Military families are once again pressing the Pentagon and private housing contractors for action on toxic mold inside base housing, PBS NewsHour reports. Service members and their dependents describe persistent contamination in homes managed by the privatized Military Housing Privatization Initiative — a problem that has surfaced repeatedly in congressional hearings and inspector general reports over the past decade.

Families say children and active-duty service members are reporting respiratory illness, neurological symptoms, and chronic fatigue tied to documented water intrusion and visible mold. The latest accounts add to a long-running pattern of complaints across multiple branches and installations.

========================================================================
## Virginia Tech Researchers Track Long-Term Mold Health Risks From Helene Flooding

Published: 2026-04-30
Category: research
Source: wvtf.org
Original: https://www.wvtf.org/news/2026-04-29/researchers-learning-more-about-long-term-health-impacts-of-flooding-from-helene
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/17157d2a984c.html
Tags: mold, flooding, respiratory illness, research, hurricane helene, water damage

Researchers studying communities flooded by Hurricane Helene say mold-related respiratory illness typically begins emerging one to three months after the waters recede. "One to three months, you might start to see respiratory illness related to mold growth," Julie Gohlke, a health researcher at Virginia Tech, told WVTF.

The team is documenting how prolonged exposure to water-damaged interiors affects health outcomes in residents who returned home after the storm. Helene's flooding left thousands of homes and businesses with hidden moisture intrusion behind drywall, in subfloors, and inside HVAC systems — conditions that can support mold colonization for months.

Editor's Note: For genetically susceptible patients (often HLA-DR / DQ haplotypes identified in Shoemaker's research), post-flood mold exposure can trigger persistent inflammatory illness even after the visible mold is gone. Standard remediation timelines do not always match the biology.

========================================================================
## Hinesville Mother Diagnosed with Respiratory Illness Amid Apartment Mold Complaints

Published: 2026-04-30
Category: news
Source: wtoc.com
Original: https://www.wtoc.com/2026/04/29/pregnant-hinesville-mother-gets-help-after-apartment-mold-complaints-apartment-provides-solution/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/93bf78649d51.html
Tags: mold, housing, respiratory illness, indoor air quality, pregnancy

A pregnant resident of Hinesville, Georgia, says she was diagnosed with a respiratory illness at Liberty Regional Medical Center last week and believes mold inside her apartment is to blame. The tenant, identified as McGee, had been raising concerns about visible mold in the unit before her diagnosis.

After local media coverage, the apartment management offered a solution to address the contamination. The case echoes a recurring pattern documented across U.S. multi-family housing, where tenants — especially pregnant women, infants, and the immunocompromised — shoulder the health consequences of building moisture problems before remediation occurs.

Editor's Note: For pregnant residents, prompt removal from a water-damaged building is the first step in any mold-related care plan. Cases like this are the textbook scenario where Shoemaker-trained clinicians recommend documented relocation alongside biomarker workup.

========================================================================
## Review Examines How the Mycotoxin Deoxynivalenol Damages the Liver

Published: 2026-04-28
Category: research
Source: Arch Toxicol
Original: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42047702/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/cf5df03af810.html
Tags: mycotoxins, deoxynivalenol, trichothecenes, research, hepatotoxicity

A new peer-reviewed review in Archives of Toxicology, led by researcher Zhu K., catalogs recent advances in understanding how deoxynivalenol — a trichothecene mycotoxin produced by Fusarium fungi and one of the most prevalent food-borne contaminants worldwide — damages liver tissue.

The paper synthesizes current evidence on deoxynivalenol's hepatotoxic mechanisms, including oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and inflammatory signaling, and surveys protective strategies under investigation. Deoxynivalenol, also known as vomitoxin, is most often studied as a contaminant of cereal grains, but its biological effects extend across multiple organ systems.

The review adds to the broader literature documenting how mycotoxins disrupt human physiology, a body of research that the mold illness community has long tracked alongside building-related biotoxin exposure.

Editor's Note: Trichothecenes are the same family of mycotoxins produced by Stachybotrys chartarum, the so-called black mold associated with water-damaged buildings. The mechanisms reviewed here — oxidative stress, mitochondrial injury, immune dysregulation — overlap with pathways implicated in CIRS literature.

========================================================================
## Miami University Students Report Black Mold in Dorm Showers and AC Units

Published: 2026-04-27
Category: news
Source: The Miami Student
Original: https://www.miamistudent.net/article/2026/04/miami-univeristy-housing-health-mold-air-conditioning-saftey
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/036242ec9786.html
Tags: college housing, indoor air quality, hvac, water-damaged buildings, miami university

An opinion column by first-year student Kenzi Perkins in The Miami Student details mold problems across multiple residence halls at Miami University in Oxford, Ohio. Perkins reports black mold spreading through both showers in her wing of Hahne Hall, with visible patches first noticed in October and worsening by January.

The piece also profiles a student in McKee Hall, Alana Molnar, who said she and her roommate were 'constantly sick for like two months' before tracing the cause to mold inside their in-room air conditioning unit. Per Molnar, replacement of the unit took roughly four weeks after it was reported. Both students cited preexisting health conditions.

The column references CDC guidance noting that living with mold can cause coughing, respiratory issues, weight loss, itchy and watery eyes, and rashes. Miami University requires first- and second-year students to live on campus.

Editor's Note: Visible mold inside an HVAC unit is particularly concerning because the system actively circulates spores and fragments through the breathing zone. For susceptible occupants — including the roughly 24% of the population with HLA-DR genotypes that impair biotoxin clearance — even short exposures in enclosed dorm rooms can drive multi-system symptoms consistent with chronic inflammatory response syndrome (CIRS).

========================================================================
## Flood Cleanup Safety: Why Water-Damaged Homes May Need Remediation Before Re-Entry

Published: 2026-04-27
Category: news
Source: MSN
Original: https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/other/flood-cleanup-safety-tips-to-prevent-illness-mold/ar-AA1Zvjeo
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/78af5ae1459e.html
Tags: flooding, water damage, remediation, indoor air quality, cirs

Public health guidance highlighted in a new MSN advisory warns that flood-damaged homes may not be safe to occupy until they are fully cleaned, disinfected, and dried. Standing water and saturated building materials create the conditions for rapid microbial growth, and visible mold can emerge within 24 to 48 hours of water intrusion.

For people with histories of biotoxin exposure or chronic inflammatory response syndrome (CIRS), re-entering a water-damaged structure prematurely can trigger or worsen symptoms. Drying alone is not sufficient when porous materials such as drywall, carpet padding, and insulation have been saturated; these typically need to be removed rather than salvaged.

Editor's Note: Mold growth in water-damaged buildings is the upstream environmental driver of CIRS as described in the Shoemaker Protocol. Even after surfaces look dry, residual mycotoxins and fragments of fungal cell walls can remain biologically active in dust and HVAC systems.

========================================================================
## Health Influencer Links Husband's 50-Pound Weight Loss to Black Mold at Home

Published: 2026-04-26
Category: news
Source: MSN
Original: https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/other/health-influencer-s-husband-lost-50-lbs-in-under-4-months-when-they-discovered-the-cause-they-had-to-leave-home-exclusive/ar-AA1WEVxM
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/d7a1e5baf969.html
Tags: mold exposure, black mold, cirs, biotoxins, symptoms, water-damaged buildings

A health influencer's husband reportedly lost approximately 50 pounds in under four months before the couple identified black mold inside their home as the suspected cause and left the residence. The influencer, identified in the report as Hannah Holt, has also described developing a rash she attributes to black mold exposure.

The story adds to a growing body of personal accounts in which sudden, unexplained symptoms — rapid weight changes, dermatologic reactions, chronic fatigue — are eventually traced to mold contamination inside water-damaged buildings. Anecdotal reports are not a substitute for clinical diagnosis, but they continue to surface alongside research on chronic inflammatory response syndrome (CIRS), the multi-system illness described by Dr. Ritchie Shoemaker that can follow exposure to indoor biotoxins.

Cases like this one underscore why patients and clinicians increasingly examine the home environment when standard workups fail to explain a sudden cluster of symptoms.

Editor's Note: CIRS associated with water-damaged buildings can present with weight changes, skin reactions, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms and is frequently missed on routine bloodwork. The Shoemaker diagnostic framework relies on biomarkers such as TGF-β1, MMP-9, and MSH alongside HLA-DR/DQ genotyping for biotoxin susceptibility.

========================================================================
## Spring Hill College responds to alumna's mold-and-asthma lawsuit

Published: 2026-04-26
Category: regulation
Source: MSN / Pensacola News Journal
Original: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/crime/spring-hill-college-alumna-files-lawsuit-against-school-over-alleged-mold-asthma/ar-AA1Zy1CZ
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/32d22be4746e.html
Tags: lawsuit, college, mold, asthma, alabama, indoor air

The president of Spring Hill College in Mobile, Alabama has issued a public response after an alumna filed suit against the school, alleging that mold contamination on campus caused her to develop respiratory complications and severe asthma. The complaint argues that mold exposure in college housing or instructional buildings was a proximate cause of her ongoing health issues.

Litigation tying chronic respiratory disease to building mold remains uncommon at the college level, where claims usually settle quietly and never reach a docket. The Spring Hill case is notable because it puts a specific physical mechanism -- airway inflammation and asthma development from mold exposure -- at the center of the legal theory, rather than relying on more general "sick building" language.

The school says it is reviewing the claims and stands behind its facilities team. The plaintiff's filings will likely turn on building maintenance records, prior complaints, air sampling history, and the timeline between her time on campus and her diagnoses.

Editor's Note: Asthma is one of the more conservatively accepted health endpoints for indoor mold, with the Institute of Medicine concluding years ago that there is sufficient evidence linking indoor dampness and mold to asthma exacerbation. The harder, more contested question is whether mold drives the systemic, multi-system illness Dr. Shoemaker described as CIRS -- which is where biomarker testing (TGF-β1, MMP-9, MSH) becomes important.

========================================================================
## Auckland landlord ordered to pay $13,000 over moldy rental linked to tenant illness

Published: 2026-04-26
Category: regulation
Source: RNZ
Original: https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/health/593451/auckland-landlord-and-pharmacist-scarlett-hong-ordered-to-pay-13k-over-mouldy-rental
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/1d8d11cae91c.html
Tags: landlord, rental, tenancy, new zealand, mold, housing

An Auckland Tenancy Tribunal has ordered landlord and pharmacist Scarlett Hong to pay roughly NZ$13,000 to a former tenant after finding the rental property suffered persistent mold and dampness that contributed to the tenant's deteriorating health. The tribunal pointed to medical documentation in which a doctor wrote that prolonged exposure to a damp, mold-affected environment was "highly" likely the driver of the tenant's symptoms.

The ruling is part of a growing line of cases in New Zealand and Australia where housing tribunals have accepted physician letters tying tenant illness to building dampness, even without a single "smoking gun" lab test. New Zealand's Healthy Homes Standards already require landlords to address moisture ingress and adequate ventilation, and decisions like this one give those requirements teeth.

For renters dealing with a damp, mold-affected unit, the case is a useful template: documented medical correspondence, photographs of visible mold and water staining, and a written record of repair requests are what tribunals tend to credit.

Editor's Note: The science underpinning these rulings has been building for years. Chronic exposure to water-damaged buildings can drive an inflammatory response in genetically susceptible people, with biomarkers like TGF-β1 and MMP-9 elevated and MSH suppressed. Dr. Shoemaker's research on biotoxin-associated illness is one of the more rigorously documented frameworks for understanding why some tenants get severely ill in damp homes while others don't.

========================================================================
## Oxford parents demand transparency after mold found at Quaker Farms School

Published: 2026-04-26
Category: news
Source: MSN / WTNH
Original: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/us/oxford-parents-ask-for-increased-communication-after-mold-found-at-quaker-farms-school/ar-AA1Zpx6Z
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/0a1327753916.html
Tags: school, mold, indoor air quality, water-damaged building, connecticut

Parents at Quaker Farms School in Oxford, Connecticut are pressing district leaders for clearer, faster communication after mold was discovered inside the building. Several families say their children have reported dizziness, headaches, and stomach pain that they attribute to the indoor exposure, and they want to know exactly where the contamination was found, what the air-quality readings looked like, and when remediation will be complete.

The complaint pattern lines up with what families typically describe in water-damaged school buildings: nonspecific symptoms that affect a cluster of students in the same wing or classroom, often appearing after rain events or HVAC issues. Parents at the meeting asked for routine moisture mapping, third-party air testing, and a written remediation plan rather than ad-hoc updates.

District officials said work is underway. Parents requested a parent-facing channel for ongoing updates and said they want a clear definition of "all clear" before students return to affected rooms.

Editor's Note: School mold cases are a common entry point into mold illness for kids. The symptoms parents are describing -- headaches, dizziness, GI complaints -- are consistent with the inflammatory response Dr. Ritchie Shoemaker has documented in genetically susceptible patients (roughly 24% of the population) exposed to water-damaged buildings. Air sampling alone often misses the problem; ERMI dust testing and visible-moisture inspections give a fuller picture.

========================================================================
## CIRSx Sets June 2026 Conference in Fort Lauderdale, Drawing CIRS Researchers and Practitioners

Published: 2026-04-25
Category: industry
Source: CIRSx
Original: https://www.cirsx.com/conference/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/e566e243c221.html
Tags: cirsx, conference, cirs, shoemaker, mold industry, continuing education

CIRSx will hold its 2026 annual conference June 25–28 at the OMNI Hotel in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, with registration now open through the organization's site. The conference brings together the physicians, researchers, building-science professionals, and patient advocates working on Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome — the multi-system illness Dr. Ritchie Shoemaker first described in the 1990s following exposure to water-damaged buildings.

Recent CIRSx programs have offered continuing medical education credits for clinicians alongside IICRC continuing education credits for remediators, reflecting the conference's central premise that human health and building health are inseparable. The 2025 conference in Tempe granted 18.75 hours of CME and 14 hours of IICRC CECs. CIRSx is also calling for new abstracts from physicians, researchers, and healthcare professionals presenting original research, clinical case studies, and emerging practices in CIRS treatment.

The organization, co-founded by Dr. Scott McMahon and Dr. Shoemaker, also operates the CIRSx Institute, which offers credentialed courses including MIR 101 (Medically Important Remediation), CIRSx Level 1: Coaching & Consulting, and Legal 101: Winning a Mold Exposure Case.

Editor's Note: Disclosure: MoldCo, the publisher of The Mold Report, is a Platinum Exhibitor at the 2026 CIRSx conference, and Dr. Scott McMahon serves as MoldCo's Medical Director.

========================================================================
## Mold Remediation Standard ANSI/IICRC S520 Cited in 2026 NDAA for Military Housing

Published: 2026-04-25
Category: industry
Source: C&R Magazine
Original: https://www.candrmagazine.com/iicrc-mold-remediation-standard-cited-in-2026-national-defense-authorization-act-establishing-uniform-mold-remediation-guidelines-for-military-housing-and-facilities/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/7e07a3a901d8.html
Tags: iicrc, s520, ndaa, military housing, mold remediation, standards, mold industry

The 2026 National Defense Authorization Act directs the Secretary of Defense to develop and implement uniform mold remediation guidelines for military housing, facilities, and other real property “consistent with industry standards,” with the ANSI/IICRC S520 Standard for Professional Mold Remediation specifically named. The inclusion is the most significant federal recognition the standard has received to date and follows years of complaints from military families about mold in base housing.

S520 has long been the leading US consensus document for how mold remediation should be conducted — including how to determine contamination conditions, plan a remediation response, and confirm successful completion. Naming it as the reference for the Department of Defense's housing portfolio creates clearer pressure on contractors to follow the standard's requirements rather than improvising scopes of work.

The current edition, ANSI/IICRC S520-2024, is the fourth revision of the standard. The 2024 update added “mycotoxins” and “ECM” (Erosion Containment Module-related terminology) to its condition definitions, introduced “airborne” as a defined condition, and called direct attention to the finding that most mold remediation firms are not correctly insured for the work they perform.

Editor's Note: For families affected by mold in military housing, a codified standard means a contractor's deviation from S520 is harder to defend. Whether enforcement and oversight match the standard's language will be the real test.

========================================================================
## Mold Remediation Market Approaches $4 Billion as Consolidation Accelerates

Published: 2026-04-25
Category: industry
Source: Restoration & Remediation Magazine
Original: https://www.randrmagonline.com/articles/89335-property-restoration-trends-mergers-and-acquisitions
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/b5b0e7728f9c.html
Tags: mold remediation, market size, m&a, belfor, servicemaster, industry growth, mold industry

The global mold remediation services market reached roughly $2.3 billion in 2025 and is projected to grow to $3.9 billion by 2033, according to industry research aggregated this year. The compound annual growth rate of 10.4% outpaces the broader $45 billion disaster restoration market, which is forecast to expand at roughly 5.3% over the same period.

Behind the headline number is steady consolidation. National platforms — including BELFOR, Servpro, Paul Davis, ServiceMaster Restore, PuroClean, and Rainbow International — have continued to acquire regional and local restoration firms, expanding geographic reach and folding mold-remediation lines into broader water-and-fire restoration portfolios. In a notable recent transaction, an affiliate of Roark Capital Management acquired ServiceMaster Brands from ServiceMaster Global Holdings for $1.553 billion, separating the franchised restoration business from the legacy pest-control operation now known as Terminix Global Holdings.

The growth rate reflects two converging pressures: an aging US housing stock with growing water-damage exposure, and rising consumer awareness of mold's health implications, which is pulling more affected residents toward professional remediation rather than DIY cleanup.

Editor's Note: Market growth without enforcement creates risk for patients. The same forces fueling consolidation — speed, scale, thin margins — also pressure contractors to cut corners. For CIRS-sensitive patients, hiring a remediator certified to ANSI/IICRC S520 remains the practical guardrail.

========================================================================
## Mycotoxin Testing Market Set to Nearly Double by 2034, Hitting $2.78 Billion

Published: 2026-04-25
Category: industry
Source: Fortune Business Insights
Original: https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/mycotoxin-testing-market-106204
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/ac44f44af113.html
Tags: mycotoxin testing, urine mycotoxin testing, blood biomarkers, shoemaker protocol, diagnostics, industry growth, mold industry

The global mycotoxin testing market is projected to grow from $1.53 billion in 2026 to $2.78 billion by 2034 — a compound annual growth rate of 7.73% — according to industry forecasts. The figures cover the full breadth of mycotoxin assay use, including food and feed safety, agricultural trade, and clinical diagnostics, with growth driven by tightening regulatory frameworks and rising awareness of indoor mold exposure.

Within the clinical segment, demand is being shaped by direct-to-consumer expansion. RealTime Laboratories announced in January 2026 that its 16-mycotoxin at-home urine panel was now available for direct consumer purchase. Mosaic Diagnostics — formerly Great Plains Laboratory — continues to run its 11-mycotoxin MycoTOX Profile through practitioner channels.

The growth comes despite serious unresolved problems with what urine mycotoxin testing actually measures in a clinical context. Urinary panels cannot distinguish between mycotoxins ingested from food — corn, peanuts, coffee, grains — and mycotoxins metabolized after inhaling spores from a water-damaged building. The tests lack validated clinical reference ranges, are not diagnostic for Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (CIRS), and have drawn caution from the CDC. Where food and feed testing is anchored by clear regulatory mandates such as FDA action levels and EU maximum allowable concentrations, clinical urine panels remain a contested space. Major medical bodies have not endorsed urinary mycotoxin testing as a standalone diagnostic for human exposure from buildings, even as integrative practitioners continue to use it.

Editor's Note: The Mold Report's position on urine mycotoxin testing: it is not a useful diagnostic for mold illness from water-damaged buildings. The tests cannot distinguish dietary exposure (food, coffee, grains) from environmental exposure, lack validated clinical reference ranges, and are not diagnostic for CIRS — the immune-mediated condition the Shoemaker Protocol identifies and treats. Patients investigating suspected mold-related illness are better served by blood biomarkers — TGF-β1, MMP-9, and MSH — the markers Dr. Ritchie Shoemaker validated across 30+ years of research and more than 14,000 patients. A urine panel result is not confirmation or denial of exposure-related illness; the next step is blood biomarker testing.

========================================================================
## Inspectors Cite Decatur Township Fire Department for Mold and Health Code Violations

Published: 2026-04-25
Category: regulation
Source: WISH-TV
Original: https://www.wishtv.com/news/local-news/mold-violations-decatur-township/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/de50531cdc50.html
Tags: mold, water-damaged buildings, occupational health, fire department, indiana, health code

A WISH-TV investigation has documented active mold growth and a series of health code violations at the Decatur Township Fire Department in Marion County, Indiana. County inspectors flagged the issues during recent walkthroughs, raising concerns about long-term occupational exposure for firefighters and staff who eat, sleep, and store equipment inside the building during shifts.

Township officials are now facing public pressure to disclose remediation timelines and interim safeguards for personnel. Firefighters represent an occupational group at elevated risk for mold and biotoxin exposure because their stations are subject to repeated water intrusion, frequent equipment washdowns, and the constant cycling of contaminated turnout gear — conditions known to support persistent indoor mold colonization.

Editor's Note: Prolonged exposure to water-damaged occupational buildings is a documented risk factor for Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (CIRS). Workers in repeatedly damp environments can develop multi-system inflammatory symptoms that persist long after a building is remediated, especially among genetically susceptible individuals.

========================================================================
## Walt Disney World Removes Black Mold from 'It's a Small World' After Mass Visitor Exposure

Published: 2026-04-25
Category: news
Source: MSN
Original: https://www.msn.com/en-us/travel/news/thousands-likely-exposed-to-black-mold-on-classic-disney-world-ride/ar-AA1WtuNv?apiversion=v2&domshim=1&noservercache=1&noservertelemetry=1&batchservertelemetry=1&renderwebcomponents=1&wcseo=1&bundles=feat-es2020-c
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/9e9448a9f260.html
Tags: mold, water-damaged buildings, public health, exposure, disney

Walt Disney World has removed black mold from its iconic “It's a Small World” attraction in Florida following reports that thousands of guests were likely exposed during recent operations of the ride. The contamination was located inside the enclosed indoor boat attraction, where visitors travel slowly through humid, low-light scenes for roughly 12 minutes — a length of dwell time that drew immediate health concerns once the mold became public.

Disney has not disclosed the species of mold identified, the duration of contamination, or the source of moisture intrusion, but the company has confirmed that remediation is complete. The incident underscores how quickly mold can establish itself in high-humidity, poorly ventilated indoor environments — the same conditions that drive mold growth inside water-damaged residential and commercial buildings.

Editor's Note: For genetically susceptible individuals — roughly 24% of the population carrying certain HLA-DR haplotypes — even short, high-density exposures to mold-contaminated indoor environments can trigger inflammatory responses. Onset symptoms typically appear days to weeks after exposure and are rarely traced back to the original location.

========================================================================
## Long-Term Study Links Early Childhood Mold Exposure to Reduced Lung Function

Published: 2026-04-24
Category: research
Source: MSN
Original: https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/other/early-life-indoor-mold-linked-to-reduced-childhood-lung-function-long-term-study-finds/ar-AA1W3R2m
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/f77398ac385d.html
Tags: research, lung function, children, mold exposure

A new long-term study has found that exposure to indoor mold during early childhood may be associated with measurably reduced lung function later in life. The research adds to a growing body of evidence suggesting that mold in homes and schools poses risks beyond acute allergic reactions, potentially shaping respiratory health trajectories that persist for years.

The findings are particularly relevant for families in water-damaged buildings, where mold growth can remain hidden behind walls and under flooring for extended periods. Children's developing immune and respiratory systems may be especially vulnerable to the inflammatory effects of mycotoxin and mold spore exposure.

Editor's Note: This research reinforces what Shoemaker Protocol researchers have documented for decades — that mold exposure triggers measurable biological responses, particularly in the approximately 24% of the population with genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR). Blood biomarkers like TGF-β1 and MMP-9 can help identify children and adults experiencing immune-mediated responses to indoor mold.

========================================================================
## Reality Star's CIRS Diagnosis Puts Mold Illness in the National Spotlight

Published: 2026-04-24
Category: news
Source: The Daily Beast
Original: https://thelooker.thedailybeast.com/mormon-wives-star-mikayla-matthews-breaks-down-in-tears-over-skin-condition/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/35b943ce02eb.html
Tags: CIRS, mold illness, celebrity health

Mikayla Matthews, 26, star of Hulu's The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives, has publicly shared her struggle with symptoms consistent with Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome following suspected mold exposure in her Utah home. Matthews reports skin inflammation, neurological issues, hair loss, digestive problems, and hormonal disruptions — a symptom cluster familiar to mold illness researchers.

Notably, Matthews experienced significant symptom relief while traveling to California and Costa Rica, with symptoms returning within two days of coming home to Utah — a pattern that researchers guided by the Shoemaker Protocol recognize as a hallmark of biotoxin-related illness tied to water-damaged buildings.

Editor's Note: While CIRS is not yet recognized as an official diagnosis by all medical bodies, the symptom pattern Matthews describes — multi-system inflammation triggered by environmental exposure and resolving with removal from the exposure source — aligns with over 30 years of peer-reviewed research on innate immune responses to biotoxins. Blood biomarkers such as TGF-β1, MMP-9, and MSH may help objectively identify this type of immune dysregulation.

========================================================================
## Infectious Disease Specialists Address Challenges in Treating Invasive Mold Infections

Published: 2026-04-24
Category: research
Source: Infectious Disease Special Edition
Original: https://www.idse.net/Fungal-Infection/Article/04-26/Antifungal-Management-of-Difficult-Mold-Infections/80351
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/92dc4f763d3a.html
Tags: antifungal, aspergillosis, mucormycosis, research

Infectious disease specialists are highlighting the growing clinical challenge of managing invasive mold infections, particularly mucormycosis and invasive aspergillosis — two conditions that can be life-threatening in immunocompromised patients. A recent review in Infectious Disease Special Edition examines new, established, and combination antifungal treatment approaches including liposomal amphotericin B and isavuconazole.

The discussion reflects increasing recognition within the medical community that mold-related health impacts extend beyond allergic responses. Invasive fungal infections represent the most acute end of a spectrum that also includes chronic inflammatory responses in immunocompetent individuals exposed to mold in environmental settings.

Editor's Note: While invasive mold infections and mold-related illness from water-damaged buildings involve different mechanisms — direct fungal invasion versus immune-mediated inflammatory response — both underscore the seriousness of mold as a health threat. Research guided by the Shoemaker Protocol focuses on the latter: chronic innate immune activation triggered by biotoxin exposure, identifiable through blood biomarkers like TGF-β1 and MMP-9.

========================================================================
## Air Force Veteran Calls for Flood Relief as Water-Damaged Homes Breed Mold

Published: 2026-04-24
Category: news
Source: The Columbian
Original: https://www.columbian.com/news/2026/apr/22/letter-flood-relief-is-necessary/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/6094ec896cde.html
Tags: flooding, water damage, mold illness, disaster relief

An Air Force veteran has written a public letter calling for greater flood relief resources, citing months of living in water-damaged conditions that have created growing risks of mold exposure and illness for affected families. The letter highlights how prolonged water damage in residential settings creates compounding health and financial burdens that many homeowners cannot address on their own.

The appeal underscores a recurring pattern in disaster response: while immediate flood damage receives attention, the secondary health crisis from mold growth in saturated building materials often goes unaddressed for months. By the time mold becomes visible, exposure may have already triggered health effects in occupants.

Editor's Note: Water-damaged buildings are the primary environmental trigger identified in biotoxin illness research. Mold growth can begin within 24-48 hours of water intrusion, and building materials like drywall, insulation, and carpet can harbor mold colonies long after surfaces appear dry. For the approximately 24% of the population with genetic susceptibility, even remediated buildings may pose ongoing risk if not properly addressed.

========================================================================
## Georgia Tenant Living in Tent Amid Dispute Over Mold-Affected Rental

Published: 2026-04-24
Category: news
Source: MSN
Original: https://www.msn.com/en-ie/money/other/distraught-woman-forced-to-live-in-tent-outside-home-after-accusing-landlord-of-extreme-negligence/ar-AA21uMcw
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/f7003aaca06c.html
Tags: housing, mold, tenant rights, water damage

A Georgia woman says she has been forced to live in a tent outside her rental home after persistent mold problems she alleges went unaddressed by her landlord, highlighting the intersection of housing-condition disputes and environmental health that affects renters across the country. The woman accuses her landlord of extreme negligence in failing to remediate mold conditions that made the property uninhabitable.

Her situation is far from unique. Renters in water-damaged properties often face a difficult choice between continued exposure and displacement, with limited legal recourse in many states. Unlike homeowners, tenants typically cannot authorize remediation themselves and depend entirely on landlord responsiveness.

Editor's Note: Prolonged mold exposure in residential settings is a well-documented trigger for chronic inflammatory responses. Research guided by the Shoemaker Protocol has shown that occupants of water-damaged buildings may develop multi-system symptoms including fatigue, cognitive difficulty, and respiratory issues. Approximately 50% of residential buildings have some history of water damage, making this a widespread public health concern that disproportionately impacts renters.

========================================================================
## ASU Students Report Persistent Illness After Mold Exposure in Campus Dorms

Published: 2026-04-24
Category: news
Source: The State Press
Original: https://www.statepress.com/article/2026/04/community-mold-dorms-69e9a8f1c0f95
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/39880a6c6c2a.html
Tags: mold exposure, water-damaged buildings, university, student health

Students at Arizona State University's Manzanita Hall and Hassayampa Academic Village are reporting chronic health problems they attribute to mold exposure in their residence halls. Reported symptoms include brain fog, persistent fatigue, chronic coughing, recurring strep throat, sinus inflammation, and mental health effects — concerns that went largely unaddressed until parents intervened.

One student who tested her room for mold at the start of the academic year described being sick the entire year with constant coughing and stuffiness. Another student received a room transfer to Barrett Residential Complex only after her parents contacted the university directly. Notably, her symptoms resolved completely after the move. ASU Housing has stated they manage air quality through visible growth inspections, humidity checks, and ventilation screenings.

Editor's Note: The symptom pattern reported by ASU students — multi-system complaints including neurological symptoms (brain fog), respiratory issues, and fatigue that resolve upon leaving the exposure source — is consistent with what researchers guided by the Shoemaker Protocol have documented in occupants of water-damaged buildings. Institutional settings like dormitories, where HVAC systems can distribute mold spores across multiple rooms, may pose particular risk.

========================================================================
## Mold Illness Highlighted Among Environmental Factors in Depression Research

Published: 2026-04-24
Category: research
Source: AMAC
Original: https://amac.us/newsline/lifestyle/10-research-backed-natural-ways-to-counter-depression/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/6305216af437.html
Tags: depression, mold illness, environmental health, mental health

A new article from AMAC reviewing research-backed approaches to depression has highlighted mold illness and environmental toxicity as underrecognized factors in treatment-resistant cases. The piece specifically calls attention to patients who feel like they have tried everything yet still suffer without answers — a description that resonates with the experience of many individuals living in water-damaged buildings.

The inclusion of mold illness in a mainstream depression resource is notable because cognitive and mood symptoms — including brain fog, anxiety, and depression — are among the most commonly reported effects of chronic mold exposure, yet they are rarely connected to environmental causes during standard psychiatric evaluation.

Editor's Note: Research guided by the Shoemaker Protocol has documented that biotoxin exposure from water-damaged buildings can disrupt multiple neurological pathways, contributing to symptoms that may be misdiagnosed as primary psychiatric conditions. Biomarkers such as MSH (alpha-melanocyte stimulating hormone) and VIP (vasoactive intestinal peptide) play key roles in neurological regulation and are frequently abnormal in patients with biotoxin-related illness.

========================================================================
## Wisconsin Doctor Warns Flood Victims: Mold Growth Begins Within Days

Published: 2026-04-24
Category: news
Source: WTAQ
Original: https://wtaq.com/2026/04/23/thedacare-doctor-warns-of-mold-damage-in-homes-after-floods-urges-health-and-safety-steps/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/ab63327c2468.html
Tags: flooding, mold remediation, water damage, public health

Dr. William Kumprey, an emergency medicine physician and associate chief medical officer for ThedaCare, is warning Northeast Wisconsin residents affected by April 2026 flooding that mold can develop in water-damaged homes within two to three days — with respiratory symptoms potentially appearing days, weeks, or even months after exposure begins.

Kumprey advises that homeowners can self-remediate small mold areas under 10 square feet within 24-48 hours, but larger affected areas involving carpeting and drywall require professional remediation. He cautioned that continued exposure in enclosed environments compounds health risks including respiratory illness and asthma.

Editor's Note: Post-flood mold growth is one of the most common sources of biotoxin exposure in residential settings. Research suggests that approximately 50% of residential buildings have some degree of water damage. For individuals with genetic susceptibility, even short-term exposure to mold in water-damaged buildings may trigger chronic inflammatory responses that persist long after the initial exposure.

========================================================================
## People Magazine Covers Reality Star's Battle With Mold-Related Chronic Illness

Published: 2026-04-24
Category: news
Source: People
Original: https://people.com/mikayla-matthews-breaks-down-as-she-gives-health-update-on-chronic-illness-11956851
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/054177519d78.html
Tags: mold illness, celebrity health, awareness

People magazine has spotlighted Mikayla Matthews' ongoing health battle with a chronic illness linked to mold exposure, bringing mainstream media attention to an issue that affects millions of Americans living in water-damaged buildings. Matthews, who appears on Hulu's The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives, has described her condition as having stripped her of happiness, citing skin pain, neurological symptoms, and the challenge of managing motherhood with four children while chronically ill.

The coverage in a publication with People's reach represents a significant moment for mold illness awareness. For years, patients with mold-related symptoms have struggled to be taken seriously by conventional medicine, often receiving fragmented diagnoses that address individual symptoms without identifying the environmental root cause.

Editor's Note: Research suggests that approximately 24% of the population carries HLA-DR gene variants that may make them more susceptible to developing chronic immune responses from biotoxin exposure. Blood biomarker testing — including TGF-β1, MMP-9, and MSH — can help identify whether environmental exposure is driving symptoms.

========================================================================
## New Study: MARCoNS Bacteria Directly Suppress Alpha-MSH, Confirming Key Step in Shoemaker's Biotoxin Pathway

Published: 2026-04-22
Category: research
Source: Frontiers in Endocrinology
Original: https://doi.org/10.3389/fendo.2026.1728408
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/0d71278385f7.html
Tags: MARCoNS, alpha-MSH, Shoemaker Protocol, CIRS, biotoxin pathway, research

A new peer-reviewed study published in Frontiers in Endocrinology provides the first direct evidence that MARCoNS (Multiple Antibiotic Resistant Coagulase Negative Staphylococci) bacteria suppress alpha-MSH production through specific enzymatic degradation. The research identifies the molecular mechanism by which these nasal biofilm-forming bacteria break down alpha-MSH, a key regulatory peptide in the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. This finding validates a critical node in Dr. Ritchie Shoemaker's biotoxin pathway model, which has long proposed that MARCoNS colonization in mold-exposed patients leads to MSH suppression, driving downstream dysregulation of cytokines, hormones, and immune function. The study's authors note that this mechanism may explain why MARCoNS-positive patients often present with treatment-resistant symptoms including fatigue, cognitive impairment, and hormonal imbalances.

Editor's Note: This study provides direct molecular evidence for a mechanism long proposed by Dr. Ritchie Shoemaker — that MARCoNS bacteria in the nasal passages of mold-exposed patients actively degrade alpha-MSH, a master regulatory peptide. In the Shoemaker biotoxin pathway, MSH suppression is a critical downstream effect of biotoxin exposure in genetically susceptible individuals (HLA-DR positive), leading to dysregulated cytokines (TGF-β1, MMP-9, C4a), hormonal disruption, and chronic inflammation. This is the first peer-reviewed confirmation of the specific enzymatic pathway by which MARCoNS achieve this suppression. For patients currently in the Shoemaker Protocol, this research reinforces why MARCoNS eradication (typically via BEG spray) is an essential early step before VIP nasal spray can be effective.

========================================================================
## Trade Medical Publication Frames Mold Toxicity as 'Expanding Clinical Frontier'

Published: 2026-04-20
Category: news
Source: MedicalExpo e-Magazine
Original: https://emag.medicalexpo.com/mold-toxicity-the-expanding-clinical-frontier-in-inflammatory-and-immune-disorders/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/e9dfc3f29309.html
Tags: cirs, biotoxin, mold toxicity, diagnostics, hla-dr, shoemaker, mmp-9

MedicalExpo e-Magazine, a trade publication aimed at medical professionals, published a featured article on April 20, 2026 framing mold toxicity as an "expanding clinical frontier" in inflammatory and immune disorders. The piece argues that emerging evidence and clinical reports increasingly point to mold exposure as a driver of complex inflammatory syndromes, and calls for sharper diagnostics and integrative treatment in primary care.

The article centers on Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (CIRS), describing it as a multi-system inflammatory condition triggered by biotoxins in genetically susceptible individuals. It cites the same biomarker panel long associated with the Shoemaker Protocol — C4a, TGF-β1, and MMP-9 — alongside HLA-DR genotyping and detailed environmental history-taking as diagnostic tools clinicians should consider. Mold-derived biotoxins, the piece notes, can dysregulate innate immune signaling, disrupt hormones, and impair mitochondrial function.

MedicalExpo points out that medical and scientific journals have been documenting a rising prevalence of mold-related health problems since at least 2007, and references public discussion sparked by Canadian psychologist Jordan Peterson's account of mold-linked symptoms. While the article acknowledges ongoing controversy in mainstream medicine, it argues the underlying pathophysiology of biotoxin-driven illness warrants serious clinical evaluation.

Editor's Note: The article references the same inflammatory markers — C4a, TGF-β1, and MMP-9 — and HLA-DR genotyping framework that Dr. Ritchie Shoemaker has used in CIRS diagnostics for nearly two decades.

========================================================================
## Jordan Peterson's Health Decline Linked to Mold Exposure and CIRS Flare-Up

Published: 2026-04-19
Category: news
Source: Timesnownews
Original: https://www.timesnownews.com/health/canadian-author-jordan-peterson-faces-severe-akathisia-what-is-the-lesser-known-psych-med-linked-condition-article-154111000
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/3848c4e074ce.html
Tags: biotoxin illness, CIRS, mold news, mold allergies, immune dysfunction

Jordan Peterson experienced a health decline last summer following what his family describes as a flare-up likely triggered by mold exposure and stress. The condition referenced is mold-related illness involving an innate immune response that may be activated in genetically susceptible individuals following exposure to water-damaged buildings. The information was shared publicly by Peterson's family to explain the worsening of his condition.

Mold-related illness is not a mold allergy or fungal infection. It may involve activation of the innate immune system in response to biotoxins found in water-damaged environments. The family's account links both environmental exposure and physiological stress as contributing factors to the flare-up.

For people managing mold-related illness, this case draws public attention to the possibility that symptoms may worsen following re-exposure or significant stress. The Peterson family's disclosure may help others recognize that multi-system health decline, when linked to building exposure, may have a documented biological basis.

========================================================================
## Michigan Inmates Sick From Prison Mold Seek Gov. Whitmer's Intervention

Published: 2026-04-19
Category: news
Source: Aol
Original: https://www.aol.com/news/advocates-huron-valley-correctional-facility-012525226.html
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/4b21d70e3952.html
Tags: office mold, mold remediation, mold in schools, mold news

Incarcerated women at a Michigan state prison are reporting illness they attribute to mold conditions within the facility, prompting calls for Governor Gretchen Whitmer to intervene. The inmates, housed at a women's correctional institution, are seeking direct action from the governor's office to address what they describe as ongoing exposure to mold in their living environment.

The available source details are limited, but the situation reflects a broader pattern of mold-related health complaints emerging from institutional settings, including prisons, schools, and government-owned buildings. Incarcerated populations face compounded risk because they cannot relocate away from a water-damaged building, and institutional maintenance backlogs often allow moisture problems to persist for extended periods before remediation occurs.

For people concerned about mold exposure, this case underscores the intersection of indoor environmental quality and institutional accountability. When occupants have no ability to leave a building voluntarily, the responsibility for maintaining safe air quality falls entirely on the managing authority. Whether or not a formal health investigation follows, the inmates' appeal to the governor signals that standard facility response channels have not resolved the concern.

========================================================================
## Mold Species Identified Through Volatile Compound Analysis

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: Scholar / Wiley Online Library
Original: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1155/ina/9350601
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/4f6462b3ce58.html
Tags: biomarker, study, Google Scholar, mold research, mold health study, aspergillus, mold biomarkers

A 2025 study published in an indoor environment journal identified consistent volatile chemical markers for the mold species Aspergillus versicolor across different building materials, including pine and gypsum. These biomarkers held up regardless of the surface the mold was growing on, which could help investigators identify this species even when the substrate in a contaminated space is unknown. For occupants and clinicians, this type of research moves the field closer to more reliable, non-invasive mold identification methods.

Editor's Note: Aspergillus versicolor is a common indoor mold associated with moisture damage and is a known mycotoxin producer; accurate identification matters for remediation planning and patient exposure assessment.

========================================================================
## Novel Biomarkers Help Assess Mold Exposure in Asthmatic Children

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: Scholar / Wiley Online Library
Original: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/pai.70173
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/4f57d9fad231.html
Tags: biomarker, study, Google Scholar, mold research, mold health study, asthma, mold allergies, mold children health

A 2025 study published in Pediatric Allergy and Immunology examined biological markers that may help clinicians more accurately assess mold exposure in children with asthma. Researchers found that no single biomarker is sufficient on its own; rather, a combined mVOC biomarker index offers a stronger, more reliable indication of mold exposure. These findings suggest that a multi-marker approach may give healthcare providers a clearer picture of environmental mold burden in vulnerable pediatric patients.

Editor's Note: This study underscores why thorough environmental and clinical assessment—not a single test—remains the standard for evaluating mold-related illness in children.

========================================================================
## Mold Volatile Compounds Trigger Toxicity in Office Building

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: Scholar / probiologists.com
Original: https://www.probiologists.com/article/role-of-microbial-volatile-compounds-mvocs-in-toxicity-from-molds-infested-buildings-a-case-report
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/fe754ecd550b.html
Tags: mycotoxin exposure, Google Scholar, office mold, mold research, mycotoxin, mold health study, WHO

A peer-reviewed case report documents a 43-year-old man with health symptoms following mold exposure in an office building. The case suggests volatile organic compounds emitted by mold may warrant investigation alongside more commonly studied exposures like spores, hyphal fragments, or mycotoxins. Research exploring mold-related health impacts continues to examine multiple exposure pathways. This account has not been independently verified by The Mold Report.

Editor's Note: This case underscores why thorough environmental and toxicological workups matter — source attribution in mold illness is rarely straightforward.

## Indoor Mold and Mycotoxins: Health Risks Explained

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: Scholar / probiologists.com
Original: https://www.probiologists.com/article/molds-and-mycotoxins-indoors-ii-toxicological-perspective
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/b0faf593934a.html
Tags: mycotoxin exposure, Google Scholar, mold research, mold health study, mycotoxin

A 2025 review published in the Archives of Clinical Toxicology examines the specific molds, mycotoxins, and bacteria most commonly found in buildings and most relevant to occupant health. The authors identify the primary fungal species and their toxic byproducts that clinicians and building investigators should understand when assessing indoor exposure risk. This peer-reviewed work provides a structured reference for evaluating mold-related health concerns in residential and commercial environments.

Editor's Note: Peer-reviewed toxicology literature on mycotoxin exposure remains an important foundation for informed clinical and remediation decisions.

## Inhaled Black Mold Toxicity Study in Mice Reveals Health Effects

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: PubMed / Toxic Rep Ser
Original: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39441587/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/7b761cfcb022.html
Tags: study, Stachybotrys, mold research, mold health study, black mold, stachybotrys

A National Toxicology Program study examined the health effects of inhaled Stachybotrys chartarum — commonly known as black mold — in a mouse model. Researchers administered the mold by inhalation to B6C3F1/N mice and documented the resulting toxic responses. The findings contribute to the foundational science needed to better understand human health risks from mold exposure.

Editor's Note: Stachybotrys chartarum is among the most clinically significant indoor molds and has been a focus of occupant health concerns for decades.

========================================================================
## Aspergillus Species Pose Growing Health Risks, Study Finds

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: PubMed / J Infect Public Health
Original: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41353833/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/9d03b2bbfe76.html
Tags: aspergillus, Aspergillus, mold research, mold health study

A peer-reviewed study in the Journal of Infection and Public Health provides a systematic comparison of three clinically significant Aspergillus species — A. fumigatus, A. flavus, and A. niger — examining how each causes disease, its resistance patterns, and its broader implications for public health. The findings underscore that Aspergillus-related illness is not a single, uniform threat but a spectrum of risk shaped by which species is present and how it behaves in vulnerable individuals. Understanding these distinctions is essential for patients, caregivers, and clinicians navigating mold-related health concerns.

Editor's Note: Antifungal resistance among Aspergillus species is an area of active concern; patients managing chronic mold exposure should discuss species-specific risks with their treating physician.

========================================================================
## Mold in Australian Homes Linked to Health and Social Problems

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: PubMed / Public Health Res Pract
Original: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40479519/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/4977bfb7b6f7.html
Tags: mold in housing, study, mold research, mold health study, housing

An Australian qualitative study examined the health and social consequences experienced by residents living in mold-affected homes, finding that exposure extends well beyond physical symptoms to affect daily functioning and quality of life. Researchers Gatto, Li, and Bentley documented how mold-compromised housing creates compounding burdens for occupants, underscoring the need for housing policy and clinical practice to treat mold exposure as a serious public health concern.

Editor's Note: Published in Public Health Research & Practice, this study adds to a growing body of international evidence that mold in residential settings carries meaningful human costs beyond property damage.

========================================================================
## Biomarkers Distinguish ME/CFS From Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: PubMed / Int J Mol Sci
Original: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40806417/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/52e57df145ca.html
Tags: biomarker, mold research, mold health study, mold testing, CIRS, chronic fatigue, mold biomarkers

A study published in the International Journal of Molecular Sciences examines how researchers are investigating specific biomarkers — ranging from visual contrast sensitivity testing to transcriptomic analysis — as potential tools to help differentiate between Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (CIRS), a condition studied by researchers, and Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS). Research suggests that accurate differentiation between these two conditions may be important because they appear to involve different physiological mechanisms. This research adds to a field where researchers continue to seek improved diagnostic clarity for both patients and providers. Note: This account has not been independently verified by The Mold Report.

Editor's Note: Proper diagnosis is the foundation of effective treatment — work like this moves us closer to giving patients the right answer, not just an answer.

========================================================================
## Mold and Dampness Linked to Fatigue, Study Review Shows

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: PubMed / Environ Anal Health Toxicol
Original: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41265402/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/00639faab62a.html
Tags: chronic fatigue, mold research, respiratory illness, mold health study

A systematic review of peer-reviewed literature published between 2011 and 2018 found consistent associations between exposure to mold or damp indoor environments and the experience of fatigue. The review, conducted by Dooley and McMahon and published in Environmental Analysis, Health and Toxicology, adds to a growing body of evidence that indoor mold and dampness carry meaningful health consequences beyond respiratory symptoms. Clinicians and patients investigating unexplained fatigue should consider the indoor environment as a contributing factor worth thorough evaluation.

Editor's Note: Systematic reviews synthesize multiple studies, making their findings more reliable than any single study alone — this one deserves attention from both clinicians and building professionals.

========================================================================
## Homeowners Combat Mold With Microbiome Engineering

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: PubMed / Front Public Health
Original: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41674531/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/77c239915579.html
Tags: mold research, mold health study

A study published in Frontiers in Public Health examined how homeowners experience household mold problems and what they think about using microbiome engineering as a potential mitigation strategy. Researchers Barry N, Landreville KD, and Blackwood D gathered perspectives from residents navigating mold in their homes, offering insight into both the human toll of mold exposure and openness to emerging biological interventions. Understanding how affected individuals perceive new approaches is an important step in developing solutions that are trustworthy and practical for real households.

Editor's Note: Microbiome engineering for mold control is still in early stages; homeowners should continue relying on established remediation standards while this research matures.

## Hospitals Combat Mold Infestations to Protect Patient Safety

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: PubMed / GMS Hyg Infect Control
Original: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41438831/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/90199dfcbc42.html
Tags: hospital mold, mold research, mold remediation, mold health study

A peer-reviewed paper in GMS Hygiene and Infection Control examines how hospitals and medical facilities identify and manage mold infestations, with a focus on protecting vulnerable patients from fungal exposure risks. The authors outline practical approaches to remediation and prevention within clinical environments where immunocompromised individuals face the greatest harm.

Editor's Note: Mold management in healthcare settings carries higher stakes than in residential or commercial buildings — infection risk for at-risk patients makes prompt, methodical response essential.

## Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome: Evidence for Treatment Efficacy

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: research
Source: PubMed / Ann Med Surg (Lond)
Original: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39649915/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/7c20d4d71ec0.html
Tags: treatment, biotoxin illness, mold research, mold health study, CIRS

A peer-reviewed paper published in Annals of Medicine and Surgery examines research on treatments related to Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (CIRS), a condition studied by researchers in connection with mold and biotoxin exposure. Authors Dooley, Vukelic, and Jim review what current research suggests regarding therapeutic approaches. For readers interested in this emerging area of study, this kind of evidence-based analysis presents how researchers are evaluating various interventions. This account has not been independently verified by The Mold Report.

Editor's Note: CIRS treatment protocols continue to gain peer-reviewed scrutiny, which is an important step toward broader medical recognition of biotoxin-related illness.

## Michigan Inmate Says Toxic Mold at Prison is Making Her Sick—Lawmakers Demand Action

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: regulation
Source: Clickondetroit
Original: https://www.clickondetroit.com/video/news/2026/04/17/dangerous-conditions-at-clinton-river/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/b189bcd1c2a4.html
Tags: mold policy, office mold, mold regulations

A Michigan inmate has reported that mold exposure at her correctional facility is causing illness, prompting state lawmakers to call for immediate intervention from Governor Gretchen Whitmer. The case highlights ongoing concerns about indoor environmental conditions in state-operated facilities and the health risks posed to incarcerated populations with limited ability to relocate or seek medical care. Lawmakers have demanded that the governor's office investigate the reported contamination and take corrective action. The incident underscores a broader issue: many U.S. correctional facilities operate with aging infrastructure and inadequate ventilation systems that can support mold growth. Prisoners typically have no choice in their living environment and may face barriers to accessing medical evaluation for mold-related symptoms. This case may prompt Michigan officials to conduct facility-wide environmental assessments and establish maintenance protocols to prevent future mold proliferation. For individuals concerned about mold exposure, this case demonstrates that documentation and escalation through official channels remains essential to triggering institutional response.

Editor's Note: The original source provided minimal detail about specific mold species, location within the facility, or documented health effects. No medical evaluation or environmental testing data was cited in the summary provided.

========================================================================
## Jaipur Doctor Creates Diagnostic Tool for Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: news
Source: ETV Bharat
Original: https://www.etvbharat.com/en/health/dr-sheetu-singh-a-respiratory-disease-specialist-from-jaipur-has-developed-a-new-tool-for-the-identification-of-hypersensitivity-pneumonitis-enn26041802596
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/7ce707367a11.html
Tags: inflammation, chronic inflammation, respiratory illness, mold news

A physician in Jaipur has developed a structured diagnostic tool designed to identify Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis, a lung inflammation condition triggered by repeated exposure to organic antigens such as mold, bird droppings, or animal dander. The tool, which focuses on systematically evaluating a patient's exposure history, has received recognition from pulmonology experts internationally. For patients experiencing unexplained respiratory symptoms, this kind of standardized approach to diagnosis can be a meaningful step toward identifying an environmental cause and beginning appropriate treatment.

Editor's Note: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis is frequently misdiagnosed; tools that center environmental exposure history are a welcome development for both clinicians and patients seeking answers.

## ANU Student Housing Found Contaminated With Toxic Black Mold

Published: 2026-04-18
Category: news
Source: The Canberra Times
Original: https://www.canberratimes.com.au/story/8552395/anu-x-unilodge-lena-karmel-lodge-filled-with-toxic-black-mould-student/
Canonical: https://themoldreport.org/a/20a8089be859.html
Tags: CIRS, mold in housing, indoor air quality, black mold, mold news

A former student has filed suit against the Australian National University following her stay at Lena Karmel Lodge, alleging that prolonged mold exposure contributed to health concerns. The case highlights ongoing concerns about indoor air quality standards and institutional accountability in student housing. Research suggests some individuals report health symptoms they associate with mold exposure. Residents concerned about mold in university or shared housing should consult with healthcare providers and document their living conditions. This account has not been independently verified by The Mold Report.

Editor's Note: CIRS litigation against housing providers is an emerging area of law that may set important precedents for tenant protections in mold-contaminated environments.
